stem:
A 7-year-old boy was rescued from a house fire 2 hours ago. He has full-thickness circumferential burns to the right forearm and partial-thickness burns to the trunk, totalling 18 percent body surface area. He was intubated at scene for inhalational injury and is now in the resuscitation bay. He has received the calculated first 8 hours of resuscitation fluid per the AU paediatric burn formula.

You are reassessing the right upper limb. Distal capillary refill is 5 seconds, the radial pulse is just palpable on Doppler, and the hand is cool. The forearm compartments feel firm. The patient is sedated and intubated, ventilated with no airway pressure alarms.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 122 /min, blood pressure 102/64 mmHg, respiratory rate 22 /min ventilated, SpO2 98% on FiO2 0.4, temperature 36.6 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: right forearm circumferential full-thickness burn with leathery eschar, distal capillary refill 5 seconds, Doppler-only radial pulse, hand cool, forearm compartments firm, partial-thickness trunk burns
data_table.Investigations: intubated and sedated, urine output 0.8 mL/kg/hour, lactate 2.4 mmol/L (under 2)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Arrange transfer to a paediatric burns centre for theatre-based fasciotomy under general anaesthesia
options.rationale: Escharotomy is performed at the bedside through the eschar, which is insensate; it does not require general anaesthesia. Fasciotomy is a different procedure indicated for true compartment syndrome and is performed after escharotomy fails to restore perfusion. Transfer should not delay escharotomy. (Source: ANZBA Emergency Management of Severe Burns)
source_refs: ANZBA Emergency Management of Severe Burns

options.text: Elevate the limb above heart level and reassess in 1 hour before deciding on intervention
options.rationale: Limb elevation does not relieve external constriction from the eschar. Once perfusion is compromised, escharotomy is needed urgently; an additional hour of ischaemia risks permanent injury. (Source: ANZBA Emergency Management of Severe Burns)
source_refs: ANZBA Emergency Management of Severe Burns

options.text: Increase the fluid resuscitation rate by 25 percent to improve distal perfusion via central pressure
options.rationale: Increasing fluids does not address mechanical external constriction by full-thickness eschar; it risks over-resuscitation, which is associated with abdominal compartment syndrome and worsened outcomes in burns. (Source: ANZBA Emergency Management of Severe Burns)
source_refs: ANZBA Emergency Management of Severe Burns

options.text: Perform urgent escharotomy of the right upper limb at the bedside to restore distal perfusion
options.rationale: Circumferential full-thickness burn with reduced distal perfusion, prolonged capillary refill, Doppler-only pulses, and firm compartments is an indication for urgent escharotomy. The procedure is performed at the bedside through devitalised eschar to relieve external constriction; it does not require theatre time. Delaying for transfer or further investigation risks limb loss. (Source: ANZBA Emergency Management of Severe Burns)
source_refs: ANZBA Emergency Management of Severe Burns

options.text: Apply silver sulfadiazine dressing and arrange repeat assessment in 4 hours
options.rationale: Topical dressings address microbial colonisation, not external mechanical constriction. Delaying intervention for 4 hours in an ischaemic limb risks irreversible injury. (Source: ANZBA Emergency Management of Severe Burns)
source_refs: ANZBA Emergency Management of Severe Burns

explanation.summary: Circumferential full-thickness burns can cause external constriction once oedema accumulates, compromising distal perfusion. Indicators include prolonged capillary refill, Doppler-only pulses, firm compartments, and cool extremity. Bedside escharotomy through the insensate eschar restores perfusion and is urgent.
key_points: Escharotomy is bedside, through full-thickness eschar (insensate), no general anaesthesia required.
Indications: circumferential burn plus distal perfusion compromise.
Fasciotomy is separate, for compartment syndrome after escharotomy fails.
Over-resuscitation worsens outcomes; do not chase pulses with fluid alone.
explanation.pearls: Circumferential full-thickness burn with cool distal limb, Doppler-only pulses, and firm compartments needs bedside escharotomy now per ANZBA; elevation, more fluid, and dressings do not relieve eschar constriction.

sources.label: ANZBA Emergency Management of Severe Burns

stem:
A 7-month-old non-mobile infant is brought to the emergency department by her father with a swollen, painful right thigh that he noticed after picking her up from her cot this morning. He says she may have rolled awkwardly. The infant cries with movement of the right leg. X-ray of the right femur shows a spiral fracture of the mid-shaft.

The infant is fully immunised, growing along the 25th centile, and on review of the parent-held record she has been seen for two previous emergency department attendances in the last 4 months: one for a frenulum tear and one for a bruise on her cheek. The father attends alone and is reluctant to provide a clear narrative about the cot event. Mother is not contactable.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 126 /min, blood pressure 92/56 mmHg, respiratory rate 32 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: tender right mid-thigh with swelling, otherwise well-grown infant, no other obvious external injury on full exposed examination, no torn frenulum currently visible
data_table.Investigations: right femur x-ray spiral mid-shaft fracture, full blood count haemoglobin 110 g/L (105-135) platelets 282 x10^9/L (150-450), coagulation profile within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Admit and arrange skeletal survey, ophthalmology review, hospital child protection team and statutory notification
options.rationale: A femoral fracture in a non-mobile infant has a high specificity for inflicted injury. The work-up includes admission for safety, full skeletal survey within 24 hours, ophthalmology for retinal haemorrhage screening, neuroimaging if indicated by age and clinical features, coagulation studies, and mandatory notification to the statutory child protection authority. The hospital child protection team coordinates this. (Source: RCH CPG Child abuse - physical; Australian Mandatory Reporting Frameworks)

options.text: Apply a long-leg cast under procedural sedation in the emergency department and discharge with GP follow-up in 1 week
options.rationale: Discharging without safeguarding workup or notification ignores a strong red flag (femoral fracture in non-mobile infant). The fracture also alone is not the indication for early discharge; the safety risk is. (Source: RCH CPG Child abuse - physical)

options.text: Send a urinary catecholamine sample to exclude phaeochromocytoma as a cause of fragile vessels and apparent bruising
options.rationale: Phaeochromocytoma does not cause fragile bones and is not part of the differential for an isolated femoral spiral fracture. Investigation should be guided by the differential of organic causes of bone fragility, not by unrelated rare diseases. (Source: RCH CPG Child abuse - physical)

options.text: Refer to orthopaedics for elective fracture clinic follow-up and arrange outpatient social work review within 2 weeks
options.rationale: Outpatient follow-up does not protect the child or initiate the safeguarding pathway. Suspected non-accidental injury requires inpatient assessment to coordinate skeletal survey and child protection involvement. (Source: RCH CPG Child abuse - physical)

options.text: Request a haematology consultation to exclude osteogenesis imperfecta as a unifying diagnosis before notification
options.rationale: Organic bone disease (osteogenesis imperfecta, copper deficiency) is part of the differential and is considered during the workup, but notification of suspected abuse does not wait for these results when the index of suspicion is high. Notification and protection run in parallel with differential investigation. (Source: RCH CPG Child abuse - physical)

explanation.summary: A femoral fracture in a non-mobile infant carries a high probability of inflicted injury. The combined workup includes admission for safety, full skeletal survey, retinal examination, neuroimaging if indicated, and coagulation studies, with mandatory notification to the statutory child protection authority. The hospital child protection team coordinates the multidisciplinary response.
key_points: Femoral fracture in non-mobile infant is a major red flag for inflicted injury.
Standard work-up: skeletal survey, ophthalmology, neuroimaging if indicated, coagulation.
Mandatory notification is a legal obligation in all Australian jurisdictions.
Investigation of organic bone disease runs in parallel with safeguarding, not before it.
explanation.pearls: Spiral femoral fracture in a non-mobile infant triggers skeletal survey within 24 hours, retinal exam, coagulation work-up, and statutory notification per RCH CPG; do not delay protection for organic differential workup.

stem:
A 5-day-old breastfed term boy is brought to the paediatric assessment unit by his parents because they noticed yellow skin spreading down to his thighs since yesterday. He was born at 39 weeks by uncomplicated vaginal delivery, weight 3.6 kg at birth and 3.4 kg today (less than 10 percent weight loss). Mother's blood group is O Rh-positive, infant's blood group is A Rh-positive, and the direct antiglobulin test (DAT) is positive.

The infant is alert, feeding 6 to 7 times in 24 hours, with adequate wet and dirty nappies. There is no hepatosplenomegaly and no petechiae. Serum bilirubin at 120 hours of age is 340 micromol/L (unconjugated component 332 micromol/L, conjugated 8 micromol/L). The treatment chart for this gestation and age placed the phototherapy line at 310 micromol/L and the exchange transfusion line at 430 micromol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 138 /min, blood pressure 70/42 mmHg, respiratory rate 42 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.8 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: alert and feeding, jaundice extending to thighs (zone 4), no hepatosplenomegaly, no petechiae, normal tone and reflexes
data_table.Investigations: total serum bilirubin 340 micromol/L at 120 hours (phototherapy threshold 310, exchange transfusion threshold 430), unconjugated 332 micromol/L, conjugated 8 micromol/L, DAT positive (ABO incompatibility), full blood count haemoglobin 158 g/L (140-220 newborn) reticulocytes 4.8 percent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Cease breastfeeding for 48 hours and substitute infant formula to reduce serum bilirubin levels more rapidly
options.rationale: Breastfeeding cessation is no longer recommended for breast milk jaundice. AU and international guidance continues breastfeeding with supplementation if intake is inadequate. Cessation undermines establishment of lactation. (Source: RCH CPG Jaundice in early infancy)
source_refs: RCH CPG Jaundice in early infancy

options.text: Arrange urgent double-volume exchange transfusion because the direct antiglobulin test is positive for ABO incompatibility
options.rationale: Exchange transfusion is reserved for bilirubin at or above the exchange threshold despite intensive phototherapy, or in the presence of acute bilirubin encephalopathy. The current bilirubin of 340 micromol/L is well below the exchange threshold of 430. (Source: RCH CPG Jaundice in early infancy)
source_refs: RCH CPG Jaundice in early infancy

options.text: Commence intensive phototherapy and repeat serum bilirubin in 6 hours; continue breastfeeding with supplementation
options.rationale: Total serum bilirubin above the gestational and age-specific phototherapy line, with positive DAT indicating haemolysis, is an indication for intensive phototherapy. AU practice continues breastfeeding (with supplementation if intake is inadequate) and rechecks bilirubin within 6 hours to monitor response. Exchange transfusion is reserved for bilirubin near or above the exchange line despite intensive phototherapy or in the presence of encephalopathy. (Source: RCH CPG Jaundice in early infancy)
source_refs: RCH CPG Jaundice in early infancy

options.text: Discharge with home phototherapy biliblanket and review by community paediatrician in the next 24 to 48 hours
options.rationale: Home phototherapy is reserved for selected lower-risk presentations and is not first choice when bilirubin is above the inpatient phototherapy threshold and there is evidence of haemolysis. Inpatient intensive phototherapy with monitoring is preferred. (Source: RCH CPG Jaundice in early infancy)
source_refs: RCH CPG Jaundice in early infancy

options.text: Reassure the family that the jaundice is physiological and rebook for repeat serum bilirubin in 48 hours time
options.rationale: The bilirubin is above the phototherapy threshold and there is biochemical haemolysis (positive DAT, reticulocytosis). This is not physiological jaundice and observation alone is not appropriate. (Source: RCH CPG Jaundice in early infancy)
source_refs: RCH CPG Jaundice in early infancy

explanation.summary: Neonatal hyperbilirubinaemia is managed against gestational age and post-natal age-specific thresholds for phototherapy and exchange transfusion. Above the phototherapy line, intensive phototherapy is commenced with bilirubin rechecked in 6 hours. Exchange transfusion is reserved for levels near or above the exchange threshold despite phototherapy or encephalopathy. Breastfeeding continues throughout.
key_points: Use gestational age + post-natal age treatment threshold charts (e.g. AAP nomogram or RCH paeds jaundice chart).
Intensive phototherapy is first-line above the phototherapy line.
Exchange transfusion is for bilirubin near or above the exchange line, or for encephalopathy.
Continue breastfeeding; supplement if intake is inadequate.
explanation.pearls: Total bilirubin above the gestational and age-specific phototherapy line with positive DAT-driven haemolysis needs intensive phototherapy and a 6-hour bilirubin recheck per RCH CPG; do not cease breastfeeding.

sources.label: RCH CPG Jaundice in early infancy

subtopic_detail: Type 1 diabetes sick day rules - intercurrent illness
stem:
A 13-year-old girl with established Type 1 diabetes (basal-bolus regimen, insulin glargine 18 units nocte and insulin aspart with meals) is brought to the paediatric short-stay unit by her father. She has had vomiting and fever from gastroenteritis for 24 hours. Her father has been omitting her basal insulin because she has not been eating, and he asks whether to continue this approach.

She is alert, mildly dehydrated, tolerating sips, with no abdominal tenderness. Her capillary blood glucose is 16.2 mmol/L and capillary ketones are 1.8 mmol/L. Venous gas shows pH 7.35 and bicarbonate 21 mmol/L. She has not been able to eat solid food but is keeping down water and electrolyte drinks.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 102 /min, blood pressure 108/64 mmHg, respiratory rate 22 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 38.0 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: alert, mildly dehydrated, capillary refill 2 seconds, mucous membranes slightly dry, abdomen soft and non-tender, no Kussmaul respiration
data_table.Investigations: capillary blood glucose 16.2 mmol/L, capillary ketones 1.8 mmol/L (under 0.6), venous pH 7.35 (7.35-7.45), bicarbonate 21 mmol/L (22-32), sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management of her insulin regimen?

options.text: Discharge home with antiemetic ondansetron and advice to omit her insulin until she is eating normal meals again
options.rationale: Discharge with insulin omission is unsafe. Sick day rules require continuation of basal insulin, with correction doses for raised ketones, and clear advice to re-present if vomiting worsens or ketones rise. (Source: ISPAD Sick Day Management Guidelines)

options.text: Withhold both basal and bolus insulin while she is not eating and restart once she is tolerating solid food again
options.rationale: Omitting basal insulin is the most common precipitant of DKA in established Type 1 diabetes during intercurrent illness. The insulin requirement is often higher, not lower, during illness because of counter-regulatory hormones. (Source: ISPAD Sick Day Management Guidelines)

options.text: Reduce her basal insulin glargine to half dose because of the reduced oral intake and review again in 12 hours
options.rationale: Reducing basal insulin in the presence of raised ketones and intercurrent illness worsens insulinopenia and risks DKA. The opposite approach (continue basal, add correction doses) is correct. (Source: ISPAD Sick Day Management Guidelines)

options.text: Switch her to an intravenous insulin infusion regardless of acid-base status to ensure reliable insulin delivery
options.rationale: IV insulin infusion is reserved for established DKA (pH below 7.30 or bicarbonate below 18). This patient has ketosis without DKA and can be managed with subcutaneous insulin per sick day rules. (Source: ISPAD Sick Day Management Guidelines)

options.text: Continue usual basal insulin glargine, give rapid-acting aspart correction doses for ketones, substitute carbohydrate fluids
options.rationale: Sick day rules in Type 1 diabetes are explicit: never omit basal insulin even when not eating, give correction doses of rapid-acting insulin (typically 10 to 20 percent of total daily dose) for raised ketones, and substitute meals with sugar-containing fluids if solid food is not tolerated. Omitting basal precipitates DKA. (Source: ISPAD Sick Day Management Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Sick day rules in Type 1 diabetes mandate continuation of basal insulin throughout intercurrent illness. Rapid-acting insulin correction doses are added for raised ketones (typically 10 to 20 percent of total daily dose). Meals are substituted with sugar-containing fluids when solid food is not tolerated. Omitting basal insulin is the most common precipitant of DKA in established disease and the most important sick day error to correct in parents.
key_points: Never omit basal insulin during illness.
Correction doses with rapid-acting insulin: 10 to 20 percent of total daily dose when ketones above 1.5 mmol/L.
Substitute meals with sugar-containing fluids if not tolerating solids.
Re-present for vomiting, rising ketones, abdominal pain, or drowsiness.
explanation.pearls: T1DM sick day rules: continue basal insulin, give 10 to 20 percent total daily dose as rapid-acting correction for ketones, substitute carbohydrate-containing fluids for solid meals; omitting basal is the most common DKA precipitant.

stem:
A 13-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with 3 hours of severe right-sided scrotal pain that began at school. The pain came on suddenly while he was sitting in class. He has vomited once. He has no urinary symptoms, no fever, and no preceding illness. There is no history of trauma.

On examination the right testis is high-riding and lies in a horizontal axis. There is marked tenderness on palpation. The cremasteric reflex is absent on the right. The left testis is normal. The urinary tract is otherwise normal. The on-call paediatric surgical registrar is contactable and in the hospital.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 108 /min, blood pressure 124/74 mmHg, respiratory rate 18 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.9 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: right testis high-riding with horizontal lie, marked tenderness, absent cremasteric reflex on right, left testis normal, no scrotal erythema
data_table.Investigations: urinalysis no leucocytes no nitrites, full blood count haemoglobin 138 g/L white cells 9.4 x10^9/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Arrange urgent scrotal ultrasound with colour Doppler before deciding on surgical exploration
options.rationale: Doppler ultrasound has good sensitivity but is operator-dependent and false-negative results occur. When the clinical picture is classic for torsion, surgical exploration is not delayed for imaging. Ultrasound is reserved for equivocal cases. (Source: AUA paediatric urology guidance)

options.text: Urgent referral to paediatric surgery for scrotal exploration and orchidopexy
options.rationale: Sudden-onset severe testicular pain with high-riding horizontal testis and absent cremasteric reflex is testicular torsion until proven otherwise. Surgical exploration must occur within 6 hours of symptom onset to maximise testicular salvage. Imaging that delays surgery is unsafe; clinical diagnosis triggers theatre directly. (Source: AUA paediatric urology guidance; RCH CPG Acute scrotum)

options.text: Commence oral cefalexin 500 mg twice daily for empirical epididymo-orchitis and review in 24 hours
options.rationale: Epididymo-orchitis typically has a more gradual onset, urinary symptoms, and a normal cremasteric reflex. Antibiotic trial in a presentation classic for torsion wastes the salvage window. (Source: AUA paediatric urology guidance)

options.text: Attempt manual detorsion at the bedside and admit for surgical orchidopexy in the morning
options.rationale: Manual detorsion can be considered when there is a substantial delay before theatre, but it is not a substitute for surgical exploration; success rate is variable and confirmation of resolution still requires operative fixation of both testes within the same admission. (Source: AUA paediatric urology guidance)

options.text: Administer simple analgesia and discharge with general practitioner review in 1 to 2 days
options.rationale: Discharge from the emergency department for a high-suspicion torsion presentation is unsafe and leads to testicular loss. (Source: RCH CPG Acute scrotum)

explanation.summary: Sudden-onset severe testicular pain with high-riding horizontal lie and absent cremasteric reflex is testicular torsion. Surgical exploration within 6 hours of symptom onset maximises salvage; imaging or antibiotic trial is not appropriate when the clinical picture is typical. Both testes are fixed at surgery because of the bell-clapper anatomy.
key_points: Classic torsion: sudden pain, high-riding horizontal testis, absent cremasteric reflex.
Salvage window is best within 6 hours of symptom onset.
Doppler ultrasound is reserved for equivocal presentations.
Both testes are fixed at surgery due to bell-clapper anomaly.
explanation.pearls: Testicular torsion is a clinical diagnosis; take to theatre for scrotal exploration and bilateral orchidopexy within 6 hours of symptom onset rather than delaying for Doppler ultrasound.

subtopic_detail: Sodium valproate in women of childbearing age
stem:
A 28-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder attends your psychiatry outpatient clinic for review. She has been stable on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily for 4 years with no relapses. She tells you that she and her partner are planning a pregnancy in the next 6 months. She is not currently using contraception.

She has a documented history of three manic episodes prior to commencing valproate, the last with a brief admission under the Mental Health Act for risk of harm to others. She has not had a depressive episode since valproate initiation. She has no comorbid medical conditions. She has not previously trialled lithium or lamotrigine.

data_table.Examination: well-groomed, euthymic mood, no thought disorder, no psychotic features, normal cognition, BMI 24, no neurological signs
data_table.Investigations: current valproate level 78 micromol/L (therapeutic 350-700 not routinely targeted), full blood count and liver function within normal limits, urine pregnancy test negative today, beta-hCG not done

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue valproate and add high-dose folate 5 mg daily, reviewing in pregnancy if it occurs in the next 6 months
options.rationale: Same problem as above; the teratogenicity of valproate extends well beyond neural tube defects and is not mitigated by folate. (Source: TGA valproate-containing medicines safety communications)

options.text: Continue valproate at current dose and provide written advice on neural tube defect risk plus high-dose folate
options.rationale: Folate supplementation does not abolish the substantial teratogenic risk from valproate. AU regulators have restricted valproate use in WCBA and a pregnancy planning consultation should pivot to a safer agent. (Source: TGA valproate-containing medicines safety communications)

options.text: Cease valproate over 4 weeks and commence lithium 400 mg nocte plus Pregnancy Prevention Program until conception planned
options.rationale: Sodium valproate is contraindicated in women of childbearing potential unless conditions of the AU Pregnancy Prevention Program are met because the absolute risk of major congenital malformations (around 10 percent) and neurodevelopmental disorders (around 30 to 40 percent) is high. Lithium is preferred for relapse prevention in bipolar I disorder with manic predominance, with much lower teratogenic risk and reversible Ebstein anomaly concern weighed against ongoing manic relapse risk. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG; TGA valproate-containing medicines safety communications)

options.text: Switch valproate directly to lamotrigine 25 mg daily with stepwise titration as the primary maintenance choice
options.rationale: Lamotrigine is recommended for bipolar depression prevention rather than manic relapse prevention. Given her history of manic episodes and absence of depressive episodes on valproate, lithium is the preferred long-term agent. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

options.text: Cease valproate immediately and trial olanzapine 10 mg nocte as monotherapy maintenance instead of mood stabilisers
options.rationale: Abrupt cessation of valproate without an overlap risks destabilisation. Olanzapine is an option for acute mania but lithium has superior maintenance evidence for bipolar I with manic predominance and is the preferred long-term agent. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

explanation.summary: Sodium valproate is associated with up to 10 percent risk of major congenital malformations and 30 to 40 percent risk of neurodevelopmental disorders in exposed children. TGA restrictions require adherence to a Pregnancy Prevention Program for use in women of childbearing potential. Pre-conception planning should switch to a safer agent rather than wait for a positive pregnancy test. Lithium is preferred for bipolar I with manic predominance and has lower (but not zero) teratogenic risk.
key_points: Valproate WCBA risk: around 10 percent major malformations, 30 to 40 percent neurodevelopmental disorders.
Pre-conception switch should be planned, not at the time of positive pregnancy test.
Lithium is preferred for manic-predominant bipolar I maintenance during pregnancy planning.
Lamotrigine is preferred for bipolar depression prevention.
explanation.pearls: Switch valproate to lithium 400 mg nocte for a woman planning pregnancy with manic-predominant bipolar I disorder; folate does not mitigate the 10 percent malformation and 30 to 40 percent neurodevelopmental risks of valproate.
